history.label: Origins
history.text: Honolulu is Hawaiian for 'sheltered harbor' or 'calm port'; this former meaning is also reflected in the related Māori cognate toponym Whangaruru in the tip of New Zealand's North Island.

history.label: 11th century
history.text: These indicate that there was a settlement where Honolulu now stands in the 11th century.
In November 1794, Captain William Brown of Great Britain was the first foreigner to sail into what is now Honolulu Harbor.

history.label: 19th century
history.text: The settlement grew from a handful of homes to a city in the early 19th century after Kamehameha I chose it as a replacement for his residence at Waikiki in 1810.
After Kamehameha I conquered Oʻahu in the Battle of Nuʻuanu at Nuʻuanu Pali, he moved his royal court from the Island of Hawaiʻi to Waikiki in 1804.
His court relocated in 1809 to what is now downtown Honolulu.
The capital was moved back to Kailua-Kona in 1812.
In 1845, Kamehameha III moved the permanent capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from Lahaina on Maui to Honolulu.
Modern air travel brings, as of 2007, 7.6 million visitors annually to the islands, with 62.3% entering at Honolulu International Airport.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: Evidence of the first settlement of Honolulu by the original Polynesian migrants to the archipelago comes from oral histories and artifacts.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: More foreign ships followed, making the port of Honolulu a focal point for merchant ships traveling between North America and Asia.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: He and the kings who followed him transformed Honolulu into a modern capital, erecting buildings such as St.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: At the same time, Honolulu became the islands' center of commerce, with descendants of American missionaries establishing major businesses downtown.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Today, Honolulu is a modern city with numerous high-rise buildings, and Waikiki is the center of the tourism industry in Hawaii, with thousands of hotel rooms.
